story:
Can you keep a secret? I can’t remember a time before I met Jackson. He lived down the road, same neighborhood, same pre-K, always there—loud laugh, shaggy hair, stubborn as a barnacle. At first, I couldn’t stand him. I even “missed” a football once and kicked his shin on purpose. But he grew on me. I loved his dark jokes and the way he skipped rocks at the pond, counting rings like they were medals. By ten, we were inseparable.

Can you keep a secret? I fell in love with the boy next door. By twelve I was smitten; by sixteen, we were holding hands, skipping class, kissing under the bleachers. It felt like a movie: his parents loved me; mine loved him. We had known each other since tadpole swim lessons. By twenty-one we were in college and living together, certain the future would be easy because we had each other.

That’s when it changed. Stress turned him sharp. He grew controlling; anger came fast and mean. One night I spilled a little red wine on the carpet. He hit me. You never think, “This is the day everything ends,” especially in your twenties. That night, I learned how quickly love can turn into fear.

Can you keep a secret? The next five years were a loop of terror and apology. He shouted, gaslit, then flooded me with gifts and promises—roses, big speeches, vows to do better. My sister begged me to leave, but how do you leave the only life you know? And there were good memories, too, the kind that stick like honey: a honeymoon in the Alps, fireside nights in a cabin; the day our son was born, when Jackson’s smile looked like sunlight; the surprise library he built for me on an ordinary Tuesday. Those moments confused me into staying.

Can you keep a secret? I’m 36 and I’m tired. I see the bruises I cover, the finger that never set right, the hollow eyes in the mirror. Worse, I still see how my son used to watch us, and I’m terrified by what he learned—that love could look like fear. We tried counseling. We tried separating. Every time, he’d change for a week or two, then the storm would return—yelling, tears, slammed doors, apologies, repeat. I miss the boy counting ripples on the pond more than I can say.

Can you keep a secret? I kept performing the part: laying out his clothes, greeting him with hugs he didn’t deserve, scrubbing away the scent of someone else’s perfume, making his breakfast, pouring his coffee. I hid the evidence so no one would see how perfectly my bruises fit his hand.

He started seeing a therapist for depression. He had heart problems, too, and took a prescription that came with warnings. I knew misuse could be deadly. One morning, I tampered with his coffee. He collapsed on the way to work, in traffic, and died.

I know what I did was wrong and unforgivable. It wasn’t justice. It was a breaking point after years of fear. In the quiet that followed, I felt only the absence of terror.

Can you keep a secret? I don’t care.
